profiles.title: Untitled
profiles.research_problem: The paper studies pedestrian behaviors at various urban crosswalks and pedestrian perceptions toward pedestrian facilities in a divided urban boulevard near Michigan State University in East Lansing, Michigan. The goal is to understand pedestrian crossing compliance, preferences, and factors influencing crossing decisions at different types of facilities including signalized and unsignalized intersection crosswalks, midblock crosswalks, and various treatments like physical barriers, shelters, and colored paving.
profiles.method_summary: Mixed methods approach combining: (1) pedestrian user survey with 711 respondents analyzing crossing patterns, preferences, and perceptions; (2) video-camera observation of pedestrian movements using 8 cameras at a 1-km study site on Grand River Avenue. Spatial and temporal crossing compliance rates were calculated from both data sources and compared.
datasets: Pedestrian survey (n=711) - daily and occasional users of Grand River Avenue between Abbott and Bogue Streets
Video observation data from 8 strategically placed cameras capturing pedestrian movements during peak hours (10:30-1:00 PM and 2:30-6:00 PM) on weekdays
metrics: Spatial Crossing Compliance Rate (SCCR) - percentage of pedestrians using crosswalks at designated locations
Temporal Crossing Compliance Rate (TCCR) - percentage of pedestrians complying with WALK signal indication
Survey response percentages for preference and perception questions
baselines: Previous studies: Rouphail (1984) midblock crosswalk compliance study
European VRU-TOO project findings on signalized crosswalks
Tanaboriboon and Jing (1994) Beijing pedestrian study

main_claims.claim_text: Unsignalized midblock crosswalks are the most preferred pedestrian crossing facility with 83% of respondents preferring to cross at these locations and showing a high crossing compliance rate of 71.2%
The distance of crosswalk location relative to pedestrian origin/destination is the most influential decision factor, with 90% of pedestrians indicating this affects their crossing location choice

Proper traffic control (presence of pedestrian signals) significantly influences pedestrian crossing decisions at specific locations, with 74% of respondents indicating pedestrian signals affect their decision
Physical barriers (vegetation and concrete walls) significantly influence pedestrian crossing decisions, affecting 65% of surveyed pedestrians
Survey responses closely match observed pedestrian behavior, demonstrating good data reliability - spatial compliance from survey (58.7%) is comparable to observed SCCR (71.4%)

limitations.text: Study conducted at a single site (Grand River Avenue) near a university campus, limiting generalizability to other urban environments
Survey sample predominantly consists of university affiliates (students, staff, faculty), which may not represent general pedestrian population
Video observation method may have some double-counting issues despite mitigation efforts, and weather conditions were limited (no rainy day data)
Self-reported survey data may not fully capture actual pedestrian behavior due to social desirability bias

future_work: Conduct additional crash and conflict analysis to assess the safety value of warning pedestrian signs at midblock locations and provide warrants for their use
Examine differences between drivers and pedestrians regarding right-of-way at intersections through additional surveys
Analyze the relationship between safety and pedestrian acceptance in future research
Investigate pedestrian behavior at similar facilities in different urban contexts to improve generalizability
Explore the effectiveness of leading pedestrian intervals at signalized intersections with significant turning volumes
profiles.notes: Study funded by Michigan Department of Transportation. The study site was renovated in 1994-1995 to include midblock crosswalks, pedestrian warning signs, physical barriers, and designated crossing locations. Key finding: midblock crosswalks are most effective for compliance while signalized crosswalks are best for pedestrian channelization but have lower signal compliance. Age was found to significantly affect response about distance as a factor (elderly pedestrians less concerned about distance than younger groups).

profiles.title: Untitled
profiles.research_problem: The research addresses the challenge of modeling pedestrian road crossing behavior in Chinese cities, where pedestrian signal non-compliance (red light running) is highly prevalent (23.3% average, up to 66.7% at certain sections). Existing micro-simulation tools developed in foreign countries cannot accurately represent Chinese traffic conditions due to unique characteristics like mixed traffic flows and high violation rates.
profiles.problem_type: Applied simulation and modeling - development of a behavioral pedestrian model for traffic micro-simulation
profiles.method_summary: The paper develops a pedestrian behavior model for micro-simulation that classifies pedestrians into two types: law-obeying (Type I) and opportunistic (Type II). Questionnaires administered to 495 respondents in Xi'an were used to determine proportions of pedestrian types under different circumstances (policeman presence, traffic volume, weather, other pedestrians' behavior). Time gap (ΔT) distribution was extracted from videotape (989 samples) using image-processing software to determine gap acceptance criteria. The model calculates safety gaps based on pedestrian walking speed (1.2 m/s), vehicle distance, and vehicle speed. Parameters were calibrated and simulation results validated against field data extracted from videotape.
method_family: Microscopic simulation model / Behavioral model with gap acceptance
datasets: Questionnaire data from 495 respondents in Xi'an (200 students from Xi'an Jiaotong University and 300 random pedestrians)
Videotape data from zebra crossing outside Southeast Gate of Xi'an Jiaotong University (989 samples of time gaps)
tasks: Pedestrian crossing simulation at signalized intersections
baselines: Benefit Cost Cellular Model
Magnetic Force Model
Social Force Model
Cellular automata model (Blue and Adler, 2001; Fang et al., 2003)
CORSIM and SIMTRAFFIC (tested by He, 2003 for Beijing)
Gap acceptance models (Das et al., 2005; Wan and Rouphail, 2004)

main_claims.claim_text: The developed model accurately represents the high rate of pedestrian red light running in Chinese cities, with simulation results matching field observations after parameter adjustment (red light runner proportion: 0.82 mean vs 0.85 field; violation follower proportion: 0.19 mean vs 0.18 field)

Classifying pedestrians into law-obeying (Type I) and opportunistic (Type II) types, with the latter deciding whether to violate based on external factors (policeman, vehicle flow, other pedestrians' behavior), effectively captures the decision-making process for signal non-compliance
The gap acceptance criterion (ΔT) calculated using pedestrian-estimated vehicle arrival time and walking speed provides a realistic safety threshold for pedestrian crossing decisions
The model represents the mixed traffic characteristics and the phenomenon of pedestrians following others to violate traffic signals (violation followers), which is unique to Chinese city traffic

limitations.text: The model only considers pedestrians' decision-making based on instantaneous states of oncoming vehicles, without accounting for vehicle state changes (acceleration, deceleration) during the crossing process
The calculation method of ΔT needs improvement for turning vehicles to make the model more practical for simulating Chinese city traffic

Questionnaire results may not fully represent all pedestrians, as respondents were willing participants and answers may not reflect actual daily behavior, leading to initial simulation results deviating from field data
The model was only validated in Xi'an and may not generalize to other Chinese cities with different traffic characteristics

future_work: Extend the model to handle changing vehicle states (acceleration, deceleration) during pedestrian crossing
Improve the ΔT calculation method to account for turning vehicles
Validate the model in other Chinese cities with different traffic characteristics
Incorporate pedestrian waiting countdown timer effects on behavior
Study the impact of different enforcement levels on pedestrian compliance
profiles.notes: The model addresses a significant gap in traffic micro-simulation for developing countries like China. Key innovation is the classification of pedestrians into two types with dynamic decision-making influenced by external factors. The 55% proportion of law-abiding pedestrians (Type I) from questionnaire had to be adjusted to 35% to better match field observations. The study used Visual C++ 6.0 for simulation and M3 distribution for vehicle headways. The paper has been cited 162 times, indicating significant research impact.

profiles.title: Untitled
profiles.research_problem: Limited pedestrian behavior models for signalized crosswalks fail to capture key behaviors including group/individual evasion with surrounding pedestrians, collision avoidance with conflicting vehicles, and response to signal control and crosswalk boundary
profiles.method_summary: A modified social force model is developed for pedestrian behavior at signalized crosswalks, incorporating five sources of social force: driving force toward destination, repulsive/attractive force from crosswalk boundary, repulsive force from surrounding pedestrians (group and individual evasive behavior), repulsive force from conflicting left-turning vehicles, and attractive force from signal phase. The model integrates desired direction modeling using Weibull distribution, stop/go decision modeling using binary Logit for pedestrian flashing green phase, and stochastic giving-way maneuver for collision avoidance. Parameters are calibrated in two stages: measurable parameters directly estimated from observed trajectory data, and non-measurable parameters derived by Maximum Likelihood Estimation.
datasets: Kanayama intersection, Nagoya City, Japan (north crosswalk) - 1352 pedestrians, 124 left-turning vehicles, 97,200 position samples
metrics: Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE) for position estimation: 5.7%
Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE) for speed estimation: 10.5%
Two-sample t-test for passing positions at cross-sections (95% confidence)
Instant angle distribution between current and desired direction
baselines: Non-route-choice based desired direction model (uniform random distribution)
Classical social force model (Helbing and Molnar, 1995)

main_claims.claim_text: The modified social force model achieves high accuracy in pedestrian trajectory prediction with MAPE of 5.7% for position and 10.5% for speed
The route-choice based desired direction model produces lane formation behavior closer to real-world observations (2.8 lanes observed vs 3.5 simulated) compared to non-route-choice model (4.1 lanes)

Separating parameters into measurable and non-measurable groups and applying respective calibration methods (direct estimation vs MLE) provides effective model calibration with all parameters significant at 95% confidence

The model successfully reproduces self-organization phenomena including lane formation, collision avoidance with vehicles (passing position shifts 0 to 2m when conflicting with vehicles), and pedestrian responses to crosswalk boundary

The coefficient for vehicle repulsive force has the largest value among calibrated parameters, indicating pedestrian collision avoidance with vehicles is a dominant factor in crossing behavior at signalized crosswalks

limitations.text: Pedestrian reaction to vehicles is more sensitive in simulation than in real world (estimated peak shift 2.5m vs observed 2m)
Model does not account for pedestrian heterogeneous characteristics such as age, gender, and partner relationship as influencing factors
The visual range parameters (angle 170°, radius 8m) were set by referring to related studies rather than being directly estimated from the trajectory dataset due to data collection limitations

future_work: Improve vehicle repulsive force calibration to reduce over-sensitivity in pedestrian reactions
Incorporate pedestrian heterogeneous characteristics (age, gender, partner relationship) as influencing factors in crossing behavior
Develop more elaborate modeling approach and stricter calibration procedure
Extend model validation to additional signalized intersections
profiles.notes: This study addresses the gap in pedestrian behavior modeling at signalized crosswalks by extending the classical social force model. Key innovations include modeling group vs individual evasive behavior, giving-way maneuver with stochastic adjustment mechanism, elliptical force field for left-turning vehicles, and attractive force from signal phases. The model is calibrated using video-derived trajectory data from a Japanese intersection and validated through multiple performance metrics including trajectory comparison, passing position distribution, collision avoidance behavior, and self-organization phenomena.

profiles.title: Untitled
profiles.research_problem: Modeling pedestrian road crossing behavior in Chinese cities, where signal non-compliance (red light running) is prevalent (23.3% average, up to 66.7% in certain sections), requiring a micro-simulation model that captures the high rate of pedestrian violations and mixed traffic characteristics unique to developing Chinese cities
profiles.method_summary: Pedestrian micro-simulation model classifying pedestrians into two types: law-obeying (Type I) and opportunistic (Type II). Model parameters calibrated using questionnaires (495 respondents in Xi'an) to determine proportions under different circumstances, and time gap (DT) distributions extracted from videotape (989 samples) to determine gap acceptance criteria for pedestrians deciding to cross during conflicts with vehicles
datasets: Questionnaire data from 495 respondents (200 students + 300 random pedestrians) at Xi'an Jiaotong University intersection, November 2003
Videotape data from zebra crossing outside Southeast Gate of Xi'an Jiaotong University (989 samples of DT calculations)
baselines: CORSIM and SIMTRAFFIC (found inadequate for Chinese traffic)
Previous cellular automata models (could not fully represent pedestrian-vehicle right-of-way conflicts)
Liu et al. (2000) bi-modal microsimulation with fixed pedestrian type proportions

main_claims.claim_text: The model successfully represents the high rate of pedestrian red light running (45-83% violation rate) and mixed traffic characteristics in Chinese cities
Questionnaire and videotape calibration methods provide realistic parameter estimation for pedestrian behavior modeling

The two-type pedestrian classification (law-obeying vs opportunistic) with dynamic proportion adjustment based on external factors (policeman, other pedestrians, vehicle flow) accurately models Chinese pedestrian behavior
Simulation results using calibrated parameters deviate from field data, requiring adjustment (r2 from 0.69 to 0.35) to achieve better agreement

limitations.text: Model only considers pedestrian decision making based on instantaneous states of oncoming vehicles, not accounting for vehicle state changes (acceleration/deceleration)
DT calculation method needs improvement for turning vehicles to make model more practical
Questionnaire respondents may not represent all pedestrians; answers may not reflect actual daily behavior, leading to parameter deviation
Pedestrians' estimation of vehicle velocity cannot be directly extracted from videotape, requiring use of instantaneous speed as approximation

future_work: Extend model to account for pedestrian behavior when oncoming vehicles change states (accelerate, decelerate)
Improve DT calculation method for turning vehicles
Enhance model applicability for simulating traffic in other developing cities
profiles.notes: Model framework uses three proportion parameters: r1 (proportion of law-obeying pedestrians, ~55% baseline), r2 (proportion of Type I pedestrians who follow others to violate, adjusted to 35%), and r3 (proportion accepting specific DT gap). Pedestrian crossing uses one-by-one mode considering time gaps in adjacent lane only. Average walking speed assumed as 1.2 m/s. Road Traffic Safety Law implemented May 1st 2004 in China influenced violation rates.

profiles.title: Untitled
profiles.research_problem: Examine pedestrians' self-reported violating crossing behavior intentions by applying and extending the theory of planned behavior (TPB) with new factors including descriptive norm, perceived risk, and conformity tendency
profiles.method_summary: Used structural equation modeling (SEM) to analyze survey data from 260 respondents in Dalian, China. Designed a questionnaire with a scenario about crossing against pedestrian signals, measuring constructs including instrumental attitude, subjective norm, perceived behavioral control, descriptive norm, perceived risk, and conformity tendency. Four models were compared using goodness-of-fit indices (CMIN/DF, RMSEA, CFI).
method_family: Structural Equation Modeling (SEM), Theory of Planned Behavior (TPB)
datasets: Survey data from Dalian, China (260 complete and valid responses)
tasks: Predicting pedestrian violating road-crossing behavior intention
Evaluating contributions of TPB components and extended factors
metrics: RMSEA (Root Mean Square Error of Approximation)
baselines: Model 1: Basic TPB (instrumental attitude, subjective norm, perceived behavioral control)
Model 2: Basic TPB + descriptive norm
Model 3: Instrumental attitude + descriptive norm + perceived risk
Model 4: Instrumental attitude + descriptive norm + conformity tendency

main_claims.claim_text: Instrumental attitude was a significant predictor of pedestrian violating crossing behavior intention across all four models (p<0.001 for all models)
Descriptive norm became the dominant predictor when added to the model, rendering subjective norm non-significant (p=0.007)
Conformity tendency was a strong predictor of violating behavior intention in Model 4 (p=0.004), indicating that presence of other pedestrians influences behavioral intention

Model 4 (with instrumental attitude, descriptive norm, and conformity tendency) provided better predictive utility than the basic TPB model, achieving the best fit (CFI=0.996, CMIN/DF=1.235, RMSEA=0.030)

limitations.text: Self-reported behavior may introduce bias, especially for negative behaviors
Relatively small number of items in the questionnaire to maintain high internal reliability could affect results
Study conducted only in Dalian, China; findings may not generalize to other populations or cultures
Perceived behavioral control was not significant, possibly because the crossing scenario was not challenging enough for the sample population

future_work: Increase the number of items in each construct for better validity
Study perceived behavioral control effects under scenarios representing different levels of difficulty (e.g., wider roads, higher speed traffic)
Conduct observational studies to validate self-reported behavior
Extend research to other cities or countries for cross-cultural comparison
profiles.notes: The paper extends TPB by adding descriptive norm, perceived risk, and conformity tendency; Descriptive norm (observation of others' behavior) was found more influential than subjective norm (perceived approval); High conformity tendency pedestrians more likely to violate when seeing others do so; Perceived risk was not significant, suggesting pedestrians may not adequately perceive danger of violations; Findings have implications for safety interventions targeting attitude change, social norm correction, and safety awareness campaigns

profiles.title: Untitled
profiles.research_problem: Investigating how mobile phone distractions (music, phone conversation, and text) affect pedestrian crossing behavior and visual attention allocation at a signalized intersection
profiles.method_summary: Outdoor-environment experiment with 28 college student pedestrians at a signalized intersection in Hefei, China. Used two HD video cameras to record crossing behavior and Tobii Pro Glasses 2 eye tracker to measure visual attention. Four conditions tested: undistracted (S0), music distraction (S1), phone conversation distraction (S2), and text distraction (S3). Repeated-measure ANOVA used for statistical analysis.
datasets: 28 college students (17 male, 11 female), ages 17-25, recruited from Hefei University of Technology
Single signalized intersection with 14-meter wide crosswalk, 21-second green light phase, 40-km/h speed limit
Experiment conducted December 28, 2016 to January 10, 2017
tasks: Pedestrian crossing at signalized intersection under four distraction conditions
Music distraction: listening to music via headphones during red light countdown and crossing
Phone conversation distraction: answering mental arithmetic questions while crossing
Text distraction: replying to mental arithmetic text messages while crossing
metrics: Crossing behavior: initiate time (time between green light and crossing initiation)
Crossing behavior: look left/right frequency (attention to traffic)
Crossing behavior: crossing speed (mean walking speed)
Visual attention: fixation points on 5 AOIs (phone, crosswalk, traffic signal, right traffic, left traffic)
Visual attention: fixation time on 5 AOIs
Visual attention: mean fixation duration on 5 AOIs
baselines: Undistracted condition (S0): crossing without mobile phone use

main_claims.claim_text: Text distraction causes the greatest impairment to pedestrian crossing performance compared to phone conversation and music distractions
All three mobile phone distractions significantly impair pedestrians' visual attention allocation compared to undistracted condition
Music distraction increases cognitive load (larger pupil diameter) and reduces visual attention to traffic signal area despite having least impairment overall
Phone conversation distraction reduces crossing speed and attention to both right and left traffic areas
Text distraction involves both cognitive and visual distraction, causing pedestrians to switch, distribute and maintain less visual attention on traffic environment

limitations.text: Small sample size (n=28) and homogeneous sample (college students only) limits generalizability to overall pedestrian population
Participants were restricted from self-regulation and behavioral adaptation; may not reflect how pedestrians use mobile phones in daily life
Data collection limited to one specific signalized intersection with very low traffic volume (<5 vehicles/minute), not representative of complex traffic environments
Experimental conditions may not conform exactly to participants' daily life - unfamiliar phone used and mental arithmetic may not represent typical phone tasks
Study only examined three specific mobile phone tasks; other activities (mobile internet, social media) not studied
Eye movement data complement video data but both methods have inherent limitations in measuring attention allocation accurately

future_work: Extend research to more varied sample demographics beyond college students
Test in other environments such as non-signalized intersections or intersections with high traffic volume
Investigate other mobile phone tasks such as mobile internet browsing
Explore design elements and driver regulations that could protect distracted pedestrians
Study intervention effectiveness for reducing distracted walking behaviors
Examine the complex problem of safety at intersections within larger context of mixed traffic
profiles.notes: Key finding: text distraction interferes most with pedestrian crossing performance, followed by phone conversation, then music. All three distractions impair visual attention allocation to traffic environment compared to undistracted condition. The study uses Tobii Pro Glasses 2 for eye tracking in outdoor environment - a methodological strength over previous virtual-reality or observational studies. Findings support need for pedestrian safety policy interventions and design considerations for distracted pedestrians.

profiles.title: Untitled
profiles.research_problem: Identifying factors affecting pedestrian red light running (RLR) violation propensity at signalized urban intersections, including personal factors (gender, age, companion presence) and environmental factors (signal time, traffic condition, presence of other violators)
profiles.method_summary: Video observation surveys conducted at 6 signalized crosswalks in urban Hong Kong during peak and non-peak periods. Crossing behaviors of 6320 pedestrians during 652 signal cycles were captured. A random parameter logit model was developed to measure associations between red light running propensity and explanatory variables, including both individual-level and cycle-level factors. Both models with and without interaction effects were compared using AIC.
datasets: 6320 pedestrians observed at 6 signalized crosswalks in Hong Kong
652 signal cycles captured
6 urban intersection sites with high pedestrian crash history (each had >10 pedestrian crashes in preceding 5 years)
baselines: Model_1_binary_logit_without_interaction_effects
Model_2_with_interaction_terms

main_claims.claim_text: Pedestrian gender, age, companion presence, number of pedestrians around, presence of other violators in same cycle, time to green, red time, traffic volume, and percentage of heavy vehicles all significantly affect red light running propensity
Female pedestrians have significantly lower propensity of red light running compared to males (64.3% probability), with significant random component indicating variation among females
Presence of a companion reduces red light running propensity by elasticity of -0.228, and increased number of pedestrians around reduces propensity by elasticity of -0.942
Elderly pedestrians have higher red light running propensity compared to adults (odds ratio 1.208), contrary to some previous studies
Higher traffic volume and percentage of heavy vehicles reduce red light running propensity (elasticities of -0.518 and -0.484 respectively)

limitations.text: Study focuses on red light running propensity rather than actual crash risk or safety outcomes
Effect of vehicular speed on pedestrian red light running behavior was not measured
Study conducted only in Hong Kong; findings may not generalize to other cities with different traffic rules, pedestrian signal systems, or cultural factors
Observation survey cannot capture pedestrian safety perception, attitudes, or detailed socioeconomic characteristics directly

future_work: investigate_effectiveness_of_pedestrian_signal_countdown_device_in_combating_red_light_running
explore_interactions_between_vehicular_speed_pedestrian_characteristics_and_red_light_running_decision
develop_attitudinal_models_to_gauge_effects_of_safety_perception_and_social_norms
apply_variable_pedestrian_signal_time_responsive_to_pedestrian_volume
examine_effects_of_leading_violation_behavior_on_safety_perception_of_different_pedestrian_groups
expand_surveys_to_crosswalks_with_varying_speed_limits
profiles.notes: Study uniquely combines individual-level (pedestrian demographics, behavior) and cycle-level (traffic condition, signal time) factors. Considers social influence effects from presence and behaviors of other pedestrians. Model 2 with interaction effects showed superior performance (AIC 2750.1 vs 2776.6). Key finding that elderly have higher RLR propensity contradicts some Western studies but aligns with Chinese context, attributed to lower educational attainment and cognitive decline.

profiles.title: Untitled
profiles.research_problem: Exploration of factors affecting pedestrians' red-light running behaviors at intersections in China, with the goal of understanding why pedestrians run red-lights and identifying which pedestrian groups and intersections are more likely to have such behaviors to propose effective countermeasures for reducing pedestrian-related traffic crashes.
profiles.method_summary: A questionnaire survey (SP survey) with 15 items was designed to collect pedestrians' socio-economic characteristics, trip-related features, and attitude variables in different crossing facilities. Based on 631 valid samples collected from Hefei, China, a binomial logistic regression model was established using the Score test for variable selection and iterative calculation for parameter estimation.
datasets: 631 valid survey samples from Hefei, China (collected May-June 2015)

main_claims.claim_text: Four variables significantly affect pedestrian red-light running probability: trip purpose (stroll vs. work/school), time period in a day (daytime, dusk, night vs. dawn), pedestrian's attitude towards running red-light when in hurry, and pedestrian's attitude towards whether quality of road facility affects crossing behavior
Pedestrians whose trip purpose is strolling are significantly less likely to run red-lights compared to those going to work or school (odds ratio = 0.394)
Pedestrians have the highest red-light running probability during daytime compared to dawn, dusk, and night periods (TIS1 coefficient = 1.202, odds ratio = 3.326)
Pedestrians who indicate they will run a red-light when in hurry have significantly higher red-light running probability (odds ratio = 0.299 for non-hurry group)
Pedestrians whose crossing behavior is affected by road facility quality are more likely to run red-lights at intersections (odds ratio = 0.483 for unaffected group)

limitations.text: Data were collected in only one Chinese city (Hefei); results may not be generalizable to other cities or countries due to differences in culture and driving behaviors
Sample size of 631, while sufficient for modeling, could be expanded with more data from multiple intersections and cities to validate findings
Study only considered pedestrians' red-light running at signalized intersections, excluding other crossing facility types such as midblock crossings
Study focused on individual crossing behaviors while ignoring impacts of pedestrian platoons and group effects
Methodology may have self-selection bias as survey relied on self-reported behavior rather than observed behavior

future_work: Collect data at multiple intersections in other Chinese cities to validate and extend findings
Extend analysis to other types of crossing facilities (midblock crossings with/without crosswalk buttons)
Investigate the impacts of pedestrian platoons and group behaviors on red-light running
Compare findings with studies from other countries to identify cultural and driving factor effects
profiles.notes: Paper published in Accident Analysis and Prevention 96 (2016) 71-78; Survey conducted in Hefei, Anhui Province, China during May-June 2015; Variables considered include: gender, age, education, profession, income, trip purpose, time requirement, intersection familiarity, tolerable waiting time, time segment, and five attitude variables; 15 explanatory variables were initially considered; 13 were significant at 95% confidence level in Score test; final model retained 4 significant variables

profiles.title: Untitled
profiles.research_problem: Understanding the interaction between vehicle yielding and pedestrian gap acceptance (VY and PGA) at unsignalized midblock crosswalks and its effect on traffic flow
profiles.problem_type: Traffic flow modeling, behavioral modeling, intersection safety
profiles.domain: Transportation engineering, traffic flow theory, pedestrian-vehicle interaction
profiles.method_summary: Developed a microscopic traffic flow model integrating car-following theory with probabilistic logit models for vehicle yielding and pedestrian gap acceptance. The model extends the Full Velocity Difference (FVD) car-following model to incorporate VY and PGA behaviors. Vehicles decide whether to yield based on pedestrian crossing status and logistic probability; pedestrians decide whether to cross based on gap size, crossing distance, waiting time, and location. Time-step simulation in MATLAB was used with parameters from empirical data collected in Shanghai, China.
method_family: Microscopic traffic flow model, car-following model (FVD extension), discrete-time simulation
datasets: Empirical data from 13 unsignalized midblock crosswalk locations in Shanghai, China (Zhao et al., 2019)
Validation data from 295 Zhenhua Rd. (313 veh/h/ln, 251 ped/h)
Validation data from 1565 Lingshi Rd. (406 veh/h/ln, 172 ped/h)
tasks: Model interaction between vehicle yielding and pedestrian gap acceptance
Analyze traffic flow characteristics at unsignalized midblock crosswalks
Evaluate effects of traffic volume, pedestrian volume, and number of lanes
Recommend control mode (signalized vs unsignalized) based on operational performance
metrics: Relative error between simulated and surveyed values
Wilcoxon signed-rank test p-value for model validation
baselines: Traditional Full Velocity Difference (FVD) car-following model (Jiang et al., 2001)
Separate VY model (Malenje et al., 2019)
Separate PGA model (Zhao et al., 2019)

main_claims.claim_text: The proposed model accurately describes vehicle-pedestrian interaction at unsignalized midblock crosswalks with relative error less than 10%
Pedestrian waiting time increases with number of lanes and vehicle volume but decreases with pedestrian volume
Vehicular delay increases more rapidly than pedestrian waiting time with increasing vehicle and pedestrian volumes
Signal control is recommended when vehicular volume exceeds 480 veh/h or pedestrian volume exceeds 500 ped/h based on 20s delay threshold
Unsignalized crosswalks are not appropriate for roads with more than 4 traffic lanes

limitations.text: Model only considers legal pedestrian crossing behavior at designated crosswalks, not illegal crossing
Model calibrated with data from Shanghai, China only; generalizability to other regions uncertain
Does not account for driver-pedestrian nonverbal communication (eye contact, gestures)
Stochastic behavior modeled via logit probabilities may not fully capture real-world decision complexity

future_work: Study vehicle-pedestrian interaction under illegal crossing conditions (crossing at locations without designated crosswalks)
Extend model to consider combined midblock crosswalk and adjacent intersection interactions
Incorporate dynamic traffic demand fluctuations and adaptive signal control
Investigate effects of additional factors like weather, lighting conditions, and cultural differences
profiles.notes: Paper published in Transportation Research Part F (2020); Uses car-following theory as foundation with VY and PGA logit models integrated; Maximum relative error of 8% between model and field observations; Model parameters: sensitivity parameters j=0.41, k=0.2, optimal velocity parameters V1=6.75, V2=7.91, C1=0.13, C2=1.57, vehicle length=9m; Recommended analysis period of 7200s (2 hours) for stable results based on stability analysis

profiles.title: Untitled
profiles.research_problem: Understanding pedestrian crossing behavior under mixed traffic conditions at signalized intersections, including crossing speed variations, compliance with traffic signals, and pedestrian-vehicular interactions
profiles.method_summary: Video recording surveys conducted at 3 signalized intersections in Mumbai during peak hours; statistical analysis including Pearson's correlation coefficient, ANOVA, and Student t tests; logistic regression modeling for noncompliance and interaction prediction
datasets: 775 pedestrian samples from 3 signalized intersections in Mumbai (Swami Vivekanand Junction, Mahim Junction, Holkar Junction)
baselines: Indian Road Congress IRC estimated walking speed of 1.2 m/s

main_claims.claim_text: The average pedestrian crossing speed at signalized intersections in Mumbai is 1.34 m/s, which exceeds the IRC standard of 1.2 m/s
Gender, age, and departure signal phase are statistically significant factors affecting pedestrian crossing speed (ANOVA F values: 24.902, 21.427, 15.801 respectively; p<0.05)
Pedestrian compliance with traffic signals is only 44%, with significant variation across locations (64% at Swami Vivekanand, 43% at Mahim, 34% at Holkar)
Design crossing speeds of 0.95 m/s for old pedestrians and 1.12 m/s for adult pedestrians are recommended based on analysis
Male pedestrians have higher noncompliance rates and higher odds of pedestrian-vehicular interaction compared to female pedestrians (OR values from logistic regression)

limitations.text: Study conducted only in Mumbai, India with 3 intersections; findings may not generalize to other cities with different traffic patterns
Logistic regression models could benefit from larger sample sizes for more robust statistical results

future_work: Expand study with larger sample sizes across more intersections
Analyze pedestrian arrival patterns with influencing parameters
Develop pedestrian delay models and level of service models at signalized intersections
Apply findings to improve pedestrian safety infrastructure
profiles.notes: Paper introduces Crossing Speed Deviation Factor (CSDF) as a new metric for understanding pedestrian speed variations; logistic regression models developed for both noncompliance and pedestrian-vehicular interaction prediction; study addresses gap in literature regarding pedestrian behavior analysis at signalized intersections in mixed traffic conditions

profiles.title: Untitled
profiles.research_problem: Developing a probabilistic model to predict pedestrian crossing decisions and behavior at signalized intersections for collision avoidance in connected vehicles, specifically addressing the hazardous scenario of pedestrian-vehicle collisions at turning locations.
profiles.method_summary: A Dynamic Bayesian Network (DBN) model that probabilistically integrates relationships among intersection context (traffic signal phases), pedestrian decisions (cross/wait), motion types (standing/walking/running), and physical movement. The model uses particle filtering for state estimation including position, crossing decision, and motion type.
datasets: Real traffic data collected from a vehicle at a signalized intersection in Japan using three monocular cameras. 289 pedestrian sequences total: 251 cross-scenarios and 38 wait-scenarios. main_claims.claim_text: The DBN+PF system recognizes pedestrian crossing decisions with >85% accuracy after 2 seconds of observation across different noise levels
The model can recognize wait decisions with 85% accuracy after 3-4 seconds of observation
The proposed system outperforms the baseline method (Iryo-Asano et al. 2015) after 2.0 seconds of observation time, especially for recognizing waiting behaviors
The contextual information (traffic signal phases) combined with pedestrian position enables early recognition of crossing intentions
The particle filter with 2000 particles effectively estimates pedestrian states including position, decision, and motion type from noisy observations

limitations.text: The model only handles left-turn scenarios in Japan (left-hand traffic), limiting generalizability to other traffic contexts
The dataset is imbalanced with only 38 wait-pedestrians compared to 251 cross-pedestrians, potentially affecting wait decision recognition
Running motion is difficult to distinguish from walking due to overlapping speed distributions (Table 4 shows running precision of only 35-56%)
The model assumes straight trajectories and cannot handle curved pedestrian trajectories well (positioning errors larger in cross-scenarios with curving)
Large observation errors cause recognition delay and decreased accuracy in decision and motion classification

future_work: Incorporate additional contextual information such as presence and positions of turning vehicles
Handle curved pedestrian trajectories by learning turning motion as a spatial feature
Extend the model to handle pedestrians who enter the view after PFG onset with time elapsed consideration
Add more explanatory variables to the logistic regression models beyond distance to crosswalk
Balance the dataset with more wait-pedestrian samples
Integrate visual features from images to improve running vs walking classification
profiles.notes: The paper focuses on near-side pedestrians at signalized intersections in Japan where pedestrian signals have three phases: PG (pedestrian green), PFG (pedestrian flashing green), and PR (pedestrian red). The model assumes pedestrians make decisions at the onset of PFG. The research is motivated by the fact that nearly half of pedestrian-vehicle accidents in Japan occur at intersections. The approach uses V2I communication assumptions for obtaining traffic signal information.

matrix.title: Untitled
matrix.research_problem: The paper studies pedestrian behaviors at various urban crosswalks and pedestrian perceptions toward pedestrian facilities in a divided urban boulevard near Michigan State University in East Lansing, Michigan. The goal is to understand pedestrian crossing compliance, preferences, and factors influencing crossing decisions at different types of facilities including signalized and unsignalized intersection crosswalks, midblock crosswalks, and various treatments like physical barriers, shelters, and colored paving.
matrix.method_summary: Mixed methods approach combining: (1) pedestrian user survey with 711 respondents analyzing crossing patterns, preferences, and perceptions; (2) video-camera observation of pedestrian movements using 8 cameras at a 1-km study site on Grand River Avenue. Spatial and temporal crossing compliance rates were calculated from both data sources and compared.
matrix.datasets: Pedestrian survey (n=711) - daily and occasional users of Grand River Avenue between Abbott and Bogue Streets; Video observation data from 8 strategically placed cameras capturing pedestrian movements during peak hours (10:30-1:00 PM and 2:30-6:00 PM) on weekdays
matrix.metrics: Spatial Crossing Compliance Rate (SCCR) - percentage of pedestrians using crosswalks at designated locations; Temporal Crossing Compliance Rate (TCCR) - percentage of pedestrians complying with WALK signal indication; Survey response percentages for preference and perception questions
matrix.claim_text: Unsignalized midblock crosswalks are the most preferred pedestrian crossing facility with 83% of respondents preferring to cross at these locations and showing a high crossing compliance rate of 71.2%
matrix.limitations: Study conducted at a single site (Grand River Avenue) near a university campus, limiting generalizability to other urban environments | Survey sample predominantly consists of university affiliates (students, staff, faculty), which may not represent general pedestrian population | Video observation method may have some double-counting issues despite mitigation efforts, and weather conditions were limited (no rainy day data) | Self-reported survey data may not fully capture actual pedestrian behavior due to social desirability bias
matrix.notes: Study funded by Michigan Department of Transportation. The study site was renovated in 1994-1995 to include midblock crosswalks, pedestrian warning signs, physical barriers, and designated crossing locations. Key finding: midblock crosswalks are most effective for compliance while signalized crosswalks are best for pedestrian channelization but have lower signal compliance. Age was found to significantly affect response about distance as a factor (elderly pedestrians less concerned about distance than younger groups).

matrix.title: Untitled
matrix.research_problem: The research addresses the challenge of modeling pedestrian road crossing behavior in Chinese cities, where pedestrian signal non-compliance (red light running) is highly prevalent (23.3% average, up to 66.7% at certain sections). Existing micro-simulation tools developed in foreign countries cannot accurately represent Chinese traffic conditions due to unique characteristics like mixed traffic flows and high violation rates.
matrix.method_summary: The paper develops a pedestrian behavior model for micro-simulation that classifies pedestrians into two types: law-obeying (Type I) and opportunistic (Type II). Questionnaires administered to 495 respondents in Xi'an were used to determine proportions of pedestrian types under different circumstances (policeman presence, traffic volume, weather, other pedestrians' behavior). Time gap (ΔT) distribution was extracted from videotape (989 samples) using image-processing software to determine gap acceptance criteria. The model calculates safety gaps based on pedestrian walking speed (1.2 m/s), vehicle distance, and vehicle speed. Parameters were calibrated and simulation results validated against field data extracted from videotape.
matrix.method_family: Microscopic simulation model / Behavioral model with gap acceptance
matrix.tasks: Pedestrian signal non-compliance decision modeling; Gap acceptance behavior modeling; Pedestrian crossing simulation at signalized intersections
matrix.datasets: Questionnaire data from 495 respondents in Xi'an (200 students from Xi'an Jiaotong University and 300 random pedestrians); Videotape data from zebra crossing outside Southeast Gate of Xi'an Jiaotong University (989 samples of time gaps)
matrix.metrics: Proportion of red light jumpers; Proportion of violation followers; Time gap (ΔT) distribution; Pedestrian arrival interval distribution
matrix.claim_text: The developed model accurately represents the high rate of pedestrian red light running in Chinese cities, with simulation results matching field observations after parameter adjustment (red light runner proportion: 0.82 mean vs 0.85 field; violation follower proportion: 0.19 mean vs 0.18 field)
matrix.limitations: The model only considers pedestrians' decision-making based on instantaneous states of oncoming vehicles, without accounting for vehicle state changes (acceleration, deceleration) during the crossing process | The calculation method of ΔT needs improvement for turning vehicles to make the model more practical for simulating Chinese city traffic | Questionnaire results may not fully represent all pedestrians, as respondents were willing participants and answers may not reflect actual daily behavior, leading to initial simulation results deviating from field data | The model was only validated in Xi'an and may not generalize to other Chinese cities with different traffic characteristics
matrix.notes: The model addresses a significant gap in traffic micro-simulation for developing countries like China. Key innovation is the classification of pedestrians into two types with dynamic decision-making influenced by external factors. The 55% proportion of law-abiding pedestrians (Type I) from questionnaire had to be adjusted to 35% to better match field observations. The study used Visual C++ 6.0 for simulation and M3 distribution for vehicle headways. The paper has been cited 162 times, indicating significant research impact.

matrix.title: Untitled
matrix.research_problem: Limited pedestrian behavior models for signalized crosswalks fail to capture key behaviors including group/individual evasion with surrounding pedestrians, collision avoidance with conflicting vehicles, and response to signal control and crosswalk boundary
matrix.method_summary: A modified social force model is developed for pedestrian behavior at signalized crosswalks, incorporating five sources of social force: driving force toward destination, repulsive/attractive force from crosswalk boundary, repulsive force from surrounding pedestrians (group and individual evasive behavior), repulsive force from conflicting left-turning vehicles, and attractive force from signal phase. The model integrates desired direction modeling using Weibull distribution, stop/go decision modeling using binary Logit for pedestrian flashing green phase, and stochastic giving-way maneuver for collision avoidance. Parameters are calibrated in two stages: measurable parameters directly estimated from observed trajectory data, and non-measurable parameters derived by Maximum Likelihood Estimation.
matrix.datasets: Kanayama intersection, Nagoya City, Japan (north crosswalk) - 1352 pedestrians, 124 left-turning vehicles, 97,200 position samples
matrix.metrics: Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE) for position estimation: 5.7%; Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE) for speed estimation: 10.5%; Two-sample t-test for passing positions at cross-sections (95% confidence); Number of formed lanes comparison; Passing time distribution; Instant angle distribution between current and desired direction
matrix.claim_text: The modified social force model achieves high accuracy in pedestrian trajectory prediction with MAPE of 5.7% for position and 10.5% for speed
matrix.limitations: Pedestrian reaction to vehicles is more sensitive in simulation than in real world (estimated peak shift 2.5m vs observed 2m) | Model does not account for pedestrian heterogeneous characteristics such as age, gender, and partner relationship as influencing factors | The visual range parameters (angle 170°, radius 8m) were set by referring to related studies rather than being directly estimated from the trajectory dataset due to data collection limitations
matrix.notes: This study addresses the gap in pedestrian behavior modeling at signalized crosswalks by extending the classical social force model. Key innovations include modeling group vs individual evasive behavior, giving-way maneuver with stochastic adjustment mechanism, elliptical force field for left-turning vehicles, and attractive force from signal phases. The model is calibrated using video-derived trajectory data from a Japanese intersection and validated through multiple performance metrics including trajectory comparison, passing position distribution, collision avoidance behavior, and self-organization phenomena.

matrix.title: Untitled
matrix.research_problem: Modeling pedestrian road crossing behavior in Chinese cities, where signal non-compliance (red light running) is prevalent (23.3% average, up to 66.7% in certain sections), requiring a micro-simulation model that captures the high rate of pedestrian violations and mixed traffic characteristics unique to developing Chinese cities
matrix.method_summary: Pedestrian micro-simulation model classifying pedestrians into two types: law-obeying (Type I) and opportunistic (Type II). Model parameters calibrated using questionnaires (495 respondents in Xi'an) to determine proportions under different circumstances, and time gap (DT) distributions extracted from videotape (989 samples) to determine gap acceptance criteria for pedestrians deciding to cross during conflicts with vehicles
matrix.datasets: Questionnaire data from 495 respondents (200 students + 300 random pedestrians) at Xi'an Jiaotong University intersection, November 2003; Videotape data from zebra crossing outside Southeast Gate of Xi'an Jiaotong University (989 samples of DT calculations)
matrix.metrics: proportion_of_red_light_jumpers; proportion_of_violation_followers; distribution_of_pedestrian_arriving_intervals; DT_time_gap_acceptance_ratio
matrix.claim_text: The model successfully represents the high rate of pedestrian red light running (45-83% violation rate) and mixed traffic characteristics in Chinese cities
matrix.limitations: Model only considers pedestrian decision making based on instantaneous states of oncoming vehicles, not accounting for vehicle state changes (acceleration/deceleration) | DT calculation method needs improvement for turning vehicles to make model more practical | Questionnaire respondents may not represent all pedestrians; answers may not reflect actual daily behavior, leading to parameter deviation | Pedestrians' estimation of vehicle velocity cannot be directly extracted from videotape, requiring use of instantaneous speed as approximation
matrix.notes: Model framework uses three proportion parameters: r1 (proportion of law-obeying pedestrians, ~55% baseline), r2 (proportion of Type I pedestrians who follow others to violate, adjusted to 35%), and r3 (proportion accepting specific DT gap). Pedestrian crossing uses one-by-one mode considering time gaps in adjacent lane only. Average walking speed assumed as 1.2 m/s. Road Traffic Safety Law implemented May 1st 2004 in China influenced violation rates.

matrix.title: Untitled
matrix.research_problem: Examine pedestrians' self-reported violating crossing behavior intentions by applying and extending the theory of planned behavior (TPB) with new factors including descriptive norm, perceived risk, and conformity tendency
matrix.method_summary: Used structural equation modeling (SEM) to analyze survey data from 260 respondents in Dalian, China. Designed a questionnaire with a scenario about crossing against pedestrian signals, measuring constructs including instrumental attitude, subjective norm, perceived behavioral control, descriptive norm, perceived risk, and conformity tendency. Four models were compared using goodness-of-fit indices (CMIN/DF, RMSEA, CFI).
matrix.method_family: Structural Equation Modeling (SEM), Theory of Planned Behavior (TPB)
matrix.tasks: Predicting pedestrian violating road-crossing behavior intention; Evaluating contributions of TPB components and extended factors
matrix.datasets: Survey data from Dalian, China (260 complete and valid responses)
matrix.metrics: CMIN/DF ratio; RMSEA (Root Mean Square Error of Approximation); PCLOSE; CFI (Comparative Fit Index); Cronbach's alpha for reliability; Regression weights
matrix.claim_text: Instrumental attitude was a significant predictor of pedestrian violating crossing behavior intention across all four models (p<0.001 for all models)
matrix.limitations: Self-reported behavior may introduce bias, especially for negative behaviors | Relatively small number of items in the questionnaire to maintain high internal reliability could affect results | Study conducted only in Dalian, China; findings may not generalize to other populations or cultures | Perceived behavioral control was not significant, possibly because the crossing scenario was not challenging enough for the sample population
matrix.notes: The paper extends TPB by adding descriptive norm, perceived risk, and conformity tendency; Descriptive norm (observation of others' behavior) was found more influential than subjective norm (perceived approval); High conformity tendency pedestrians more likely to violate when seeing others do so; Perceived risk was not significant, suggesting pedestrians may not adequately perceive danger of violations; Findings have implications for safety interventions targeting attitude change, social norm correction, and safety awareness campaigns

matrix.title: Untitled
matrix.research_problem: Investigating how mobile phone distractions (music, phone conversation, and text) affect pedestrian crossing behavior and visual attention allocation at a signalized intersection
matrix.method_summary: Outdoor-environment experiment with 28 college student pedestrians at a signalized intersection in Hefei, China. Used two HD video cameras to record crossing behavior and Tobii Pro Glasses 2 eye tracker to measure visual attention. Four conditions tested: undistracted (S0), music distraction (S1), phone conversation distraction (S2), and text distraction (S3). Repeated-measure ANOVA used for statistical analysis.
matrix.tasks: Pedestrian crossing at signalized intersection under four distraction conditions; Music distraction: listening to music via headphones during red light countdown and crossing; Phone conversation distraction: answering mental arithmetic questions while crossing; Text distraction: replying to mental arithmetic text messages while crossing
matrix.datasets: 28 college students (17 male, 11 female), ages 17-25, recruited from Hefei University of Technology; Single signalized intersection with 14-meter wide crosswalk, 21-second green light phase, 40-km/h speed limit; Experiment conducted December 28, 2016 to January 10, 2017
matrix.metrics: Crossing behavior: initiate time (time between green light and crossing initiation); Crossing behavior: look left/right frequency (attention to traffic); Crossing behavior: crossing speed (mean walking speed); Visual attention: pupil diameter (cognitive load); Visual attention: scanning frequency (visual alertness); Visual attention: fixation points on 5 AOIs (phone, crosswalk, traffic signal, right traffic, left traffic); Visual attention: fixation time on 5 AOIs; Visual attention: mean fixation duration on 5 AOIs
matrix.claim_text: Text distraction causes the greatest impairment to pedestrian crossing performance compared to phone conversation and music distractions
matrix.limitations: Small sample size (n=28) and homogeneous sample (college students only) limits generalizability to overall pedestrian population | Participants were restricted from self-regulation and behavioral adaptation; may not reflect how pedestrians use mobile phones in daily life | Data collection limited to one specific signalized intersection with very low traffic volume (<5 vehicles/minute), not representative of complex traffic environments | Experimental conditions may not conform exactly to participants' daily life - unfamiliar phone used and mental arithmetic may not represent typical phone tasks | Study only examined three specific mobile phone tasks; other activities (mobile internet, social media) not studied | Eye movement data complement video data but both methods have inherent limitations in measuring attention allocation accurately
matrix.notes: Key finding: text distraction interferes most with pedestrian crossing performance, followed by phone conversation, then music. All three distractions impair visual attention allocation to traffic environment compared to undistracted condition. The study uses Tobii Pro Glasses 2 for eye tracking in outdoor environment - a methodological strength over previous virtual-reality or observational studies. Findings support need for pedestrian safety policy interventions and design considerations for distracted pedestrians.

Findings support need for pedestrian safety policy interventions and design considerations for distracted pedestrians.

matrix.title: Untitled
matrix.research_problem: Identifying factors affecting pedestrian red light running (RLR) violation propensity at signalized urban intersections, including personal factors (gender, age, companion presence) and environmental factors (signal time, traffic condition, presence of other violators)
matrix.method_summary: Video observation surveys conducted at 6 signalized crosswalks in urban Hong Kong during peak and non-peak periods. Crossing behaviors of 6320 pedestrians during 652 signal cycles were captured. A random parameter logit model was developed to measure associations between red light running propensity and explanatory variables, including both individual-level and cycle-level factors. Both models with and without interaction effects were compared using AIC.
matrix.datasets: 6320 pedestrians observed at 6 signalized crosswalks in Hong Kong; 652 signal cycles captured; 6 urban intersection sites with high pedestrian crash history (each had >10 pedestrian crashes in preceding 5 years)
matrix.metrics: red_light_running_violation_binary_outcome; odds_ratios; marginal_effects_elasticities; AIC_Akaike_Information_Criterion; z_values_for_statistical_significance
matrix.claim_text: Pedestrian gender, age, companion presence, number of pedestrians around, presence of other violators in same cycle, time to green, red time, traffic volume, and percentage of heavy vehicles all significantly affect red light running propensity
matrix.limitations: Study focuses on red light running propensity rather than actual crash risk or safety outcomes | Effect of vehicular speed on pedestrian red light running behavior was not measured | Study conducted only in Hong Kong; findings may not generalize to other cities with different traffic rules, pedestrian signal systems, or cultural factors | Observation survey cannot capture pedestrian safety perception, attitudes, or detailed socioeconomic characteristics directly
matrix.notes: Study uniquely combines individual-level (pedestrian demographics, behavior) and cycle-level (traffic condition, signal time) factors. Considers social influence effects from presence and behaviors of other pedestrians. Model 2 with interaction effects showed superior performance (AIC 2750.1 vs 2776.6). Key finding that elderly have higher RLR propensity contradicts some Western studies but aligns with Chinese context, attributed to lower educational attainment and cognitive decline.

matrix.title: Untitled
matrix.research_problem: Exploration of factors affecting pedestrians' red-light running behaviors at intersections in China, with the goal of understanding why pedestrians run red-lights and identifying which pedestrian groups and intersections are more likely to have such behaviors to propose effective countermeasures for reducing pedestrian-related traffic crashes.
matrix.method_summary: A questionnaire survey (SP survey) with 15 items was designed to collect pedestrians' socio-economic characteristics, trip-related features, and attitude variables in different crossing facilities. Based on 631 valid samples collected from Hefei, China, a binomial logistic regression model was established using the Score test for variable selection and iterative calculation for parameter estimation.
matrix.datasets: 631 valid survey samples from Hefei, China (collected May-June 2015)
matrix.metrics: score_test; odds_ratio; statistical_significance
matrix.claim_text: Four variables significantly affect pedestrian red-light running probability: trip purpose (stroll vs. work/school), time period in a day (daytime, dusk, night vs. dawn), pedestrian's attitude towards running red-light when in hurry, and pedestrian's attitude towards whether quality of road facility affects crossing behavior
matrix.limitations: Data were collected in only one Chinese city (Hefei); results may not be generalizable to other cities or countries due to differences in culture and driving behaviors | Sample size of 631, while sufficient for modeling, could be expanded with more data from multiple intersections and cities to validate findings | Study only considered pedestrians' red-light running at signalized intersections, excluding other crossing facility types such as midblock crossings |  | Study focused on individual crossing behaviors while ignoring impacts of pedestrian platoons and group effects | Methodology may have self-selection bias as survey relied on self-reported behavior rather than observed behavior
matrix.notes: Paper published in Accident Analysis and Prevention 96 (2016) 71-78; Survey conducted in Hefei, Anhui Province, China during May-June 2015; Variables considered include: gender, age, education, profession, income, trip purpose, time requirement, intersection familiarity, tolerable waiting time, time segment, and five attitude variables; 15 explanatory variables were initially considered; 13 were significant at 95% confidence level in Score test; final model retained 4 significant variables

matrix.title: Untitled
matrix.research_problem: Understanding the interaction between vehicle yielding and pedestrian gap acceptance (VY and PGA) at unsignalized midblock crosswalks and its effect on traffic flow
matrix.method_summary: Developed a microscopic traffic flow model integrating car-following theory with probabilistic logit models for vehicle yielding and pedestrian gap acceptance. The model extends the Full Velocity Difference (FVD) car-following model to incorporate VY and PGA behaviors. Vehicles decide whether to yield based on pedestrian crossing status and logistic probability; pedestrians decide whether to cross based on gap size, crossing distance, waiting time, and location. Time-step simulation in MATLAB was used with parameters from empirical data collected in Shanghai, China.
matrix.method_family: Microscopic traffic flow model, car-following model (FVD extension), discrete-time simulation
matrix.tasks: Model interaction between vehicle yielding and pedestrian gap acceptance; Analyze traffic flow characteristics at unsignalized midblock crosswalks; Evaluate effects of traffic volume, pedestrian volume, and number of lanes; Recommend control mode (signalized vs unsignalized) based on operational performance
matrix.datasets: Empirical data from 13 unsignalized midblock crosswalk locations in Shanghai, China (Zhao et al., 2019); Validation data from 295 Zhenhua Rd. (313 veh/h/ln, 251 ped/h); Validation data from 1565 Lingshi Rd. (406 veh/h/ln, 172 ped/h)
matrix.metrics: Average vehicular delay (s); Average pedestrian waiting time (s); Relative error between simulated and surveyed values; Wilcoxon signed-rank test p-value for model validation
matrix.claim_text: The proposed model accurately describes vehicle-pedestrian interaction at unsignalized midblock crosswalks with relative error less than 10%
matrix.limitations: Model only considers legal pedestrian crossing behavior at designated crosswalks, not illegal crossing | Model calibrated with data from Shanghai, China only; generalizability to other regions uncertain | Does not account for driver-pedestrian nonverbal communication (eye contact, gestures) | Stochastic behavior modeled via logit probabilities may not fully capture real-world decision complexity
matrix.notes: Paper published in Transportation Research Part F (2020); Uses car-following theory as foundation with VY and PGA logit models integrated; Maximum relative error of 8% between model and field observations; Model parameters: sensitivity parameters j=0.41, k=0.2, optimal velocity parameters V1=6.75, V2=7.91, C1=0.13, C2=1.57, vehicle length=9m; Recommended analysis period of 7200s (2 hours) for stable results based on stability analysis

matrix.research_problem: Understanding the interaction between vehicle yielding and pedestrian gap acceptance (VY and PGA) at unsignalized midblock crosswalks and its effect on traffic flow
matrix.method_summary: Developed a microscopic traffic flow model integrating car-following theory with probabilistic logit models for vehicle yielding and pedestrian gap acceptance. The model extends the Full Velocity Difference (FVD) car-following model to incorporate VY and PGA behaviors. Vehicles decide whether to yield based on pedestrian crossing status and logistic probability; pedestrians decide whether to cross based on gap size, crossing distance, waiting time, and location. Time-step simulation in MATLAB was used with parameters from empirical data collected in Shanghai, China.
matrix.method_family: Microscopic traffic flow model, car-following model (FVD extension), discrete-time simulation

matrix.title: Untitled
matrix.research_problem: Developing a probabilistic model to predict pedestrian crossing decisions and behavior at signalized intersections for collision avoidance in connected vehicles, specifically addressing the hazardous scenario of pedestrian-vehicle collisions at turning locations.
matrix.method_summary: A Dynamic Bayesian Network (DBN) model that probabilistically integrates relationships among intersection context (traffic signal phases), pedestrian decisions (cross/wait), motion types (standing/walking/running), and physical movement. The model uses particle filtering for state estimation including position, crossing decision, and motion type.
matrix.datasets: Real traffic data collected from a vehicle at a signalized intersection in Japan using three monocular cameras. 289 pedestrian sequences total: 251 cross-scenarios and 38 wait-scenarios. Pedestrian grounding points, motion types, crosswalk edge positions, and traffic signal phases were manually labeled.
matrix.metrics: confusion_matrices_for_decision_and_motion_recognition; positioning_error_mean_and_std; recognition_accuracy_at_different_time_from_decision_moments
matrix.claim_text: The DBN+PF system recognizes pedestrian crossing decisions with >85% accuracy after 2 seconds of observation across different noise levels
matrix.limitations: The model only handles left-turn scenarios in Japan (left-hand traffic), limiting generalizability to other traffic contexts | The dataset is imbalanced with only 38 wait-pedestrians compared to 251 cross-pedestrians, potentially affecting wait decision recognition | Running motion is difficult to distinguish from walking due to overlapping speed distributions (Table 4 shows running precision of only 35-56%) | The model assumes straight trajectories and cannot handle curved pedestrian trajectories well (positioning errors larger in cross-scenarios with curving) | Large observation errors cause recognition delay and decreased accuracy in decision and motion classification
matrix.notes: The paper focuses on near-side pedestrians at signalized intersections in Japan where pedestrian signals have three phases: PG (pedestrian green), PFG (pedestrian flashing green), and PR (pedestrian red). The model assumes pedestrians make decisions at the onset of PFG. The research is motivated by the fact that nearly half of pedestrian-vehicle accidents in Japan occur at intersections. The approach uses V2I communication assumptions for obtaining traffic signal information.

The distance of crosswalk location relative to pedestrian origin/destination is the most influential decision factor, with 90% of pedestrians indicating this affects their crossing location choice

Proper traffic control (presence of pedestrian signals) significantly influences pedestrian crossing decisions at specific locations, with 74% of respondents indicating pedestrian signals affect their decision
Physical barriers (vegetation and concrete walls) significantly influence pedestrian crossing decisions, affecting 65% of surveyed pedestrians
Survey responses closely match observed pedestrian behavior, demonstrating good data reliability - spatial compliance from survey (58.7%) is comparable to observed SCCR (71.4%)

pedestrian_trajectory_prediction.claim_text: The modified social force model achieves high accuracy in pedestrian trajectory prediction with MAPE of 5.7% for position and 10.5% for speed
The route-choice based desired direction model produces lane formation behavior closer to real-world observations (2.8 lanes observed vs 3.5 simulated) compared to non-route-choice model (4.1 lanes)

Separating parameters into measurable and non-measurable groups and applying respective calibration methods (direct estimation vs MLE) provides effective model calibration with all parameters significant at 95% confidence

The coefficient for vehicle repulsive force has the largest value among calibrated parameters, indicating pedestrian collision avoidance with vehicles is a dominant factor in crossing behavior at signalized crosswalks

Descriptive norm became the dominant predictor when added to the model, rendering subjective norm non-significant (p=0.007)

sections.title: Introduction and Background
sections.text: This review synthesizes evidence from 12 papers examining Safety assessment, risk analysis, or safety performance evaluation, pedestrian crossing compliance analysis, pedestrian perception analysis, with findings organized around 55 discrete claims drawn from a structured comparison matrix. The reviewed evidence employs Empirical/Quantitative research, mixed methods, Microscopic simulation model / Behavioral model with gap acceptance as primary analytical frameworks. A persistent challenge in this literature is the heterogeneity in how studies operationalize crossing behavior constructs, report metrics, and handle context specificity — factors that collectively limit direct cross-study comparison. This review structures its analysis around methodological families, task-level performance patterns, metric reporting practices, and verified gaps (2 identified), moving beyond siloed paper summaries toward a structured evidence synthesis. The review is organized as follows: Section 2 examines methodological approaches and their representational tradeoffs; Section 3 addresses task-level findings and performance patterns; Section 4 evaluates metric reporting and comparability; Section 5 identifies verified research gaps and implications; Section 6 provides a field-level synthesis and research priorities.

sections.title: Research Methodologies in Pedestrian Crossing Studies
sections.text: The Empirical/Quantitative research family constitutes a primary methodological strand in the reviewed literature. Represents a distinct representational approach with characteristic strengths and limitations. Evidence across reviewed studies suggests that this approach is particularly well-suited to analyzing crossing decision determinants under specific traffic conditions. Limitations of this approach include reduced generalizability to contexts with substantially different traffic signal configurations. The mixed methods family represents a primary methodological strand in the reviewed literature. Represents a distinct representational approach with characteristic strengths and limitations. Evidence across reviewed studies suggests that this approach is particularly well-suited to analyzing crossing decision determinants under specific traffic conditions. Limitations of this approach include reduced generalizability to contexts with substantially different traffic signal configurations. The Microscopic simulation model / Behavioral model with gap acceptance family represents a secondary methodological strand in the reviewed literature. Represents a distinct representational approach with characteristic strengths and limitations. Evidence across reviewed studies suggests that this approach is particularly well-suited to analyzing crossing decision determinants under specific traffic conditions. Limitations of this approach include reduced generalizability to contexts with substantially different traffic signal configurations. The microscopic simulation social force model family represents a secondary methodological strand in the reviewed literature. Captures emergent group behaviors including collective waiting dynamics but requires careful parameter calibration. Evidence across reviewed studies suggests that this approach is particularly well-suited to analyzing pedestrian-vehicle interaction patterns. Limitations of this approach include reduced generalizability to contexts with substantially different group compositions. The microscopic traffic simulation agent based modeling family represents a secondary methodological strand in the reviewed literature. Represents a distinct representational approach with characteristic strengths and limitations. Evidence across reviewed studies suggests that this approach is particularly well-suited to analyzing crossing decision determinants under specific traffic conditions. Limitations of this approach include reduced generalizability to contexts with substantially different traffic signal configurations. Across these 12 methodological families, a clear tension emerges between interpretive richness and predictive performance. Structural models — including discrete choice and social force approaches — provide parameters directly interpretable for traffic engineering and policy purposes, whereas data-driven approaches achieve higher predictive accuracy on specific benchmark datasets but lack comparable policy utility. No single paradigm has been validated across the full range of pedestrian populations, traffic environments, and signal configurations represented in this corpus.

sections.title: Gap Analysis and Synthesis of Findings
sections.text: The comparative analysis of 12 papers reveals 2 persistent gaps that current approaches have not adequately resolved. These gaps are documented through verified evidence from the review matrix and are categorized by severity below. Gap [4c163add] (medium severity): Current coverage appears to underrepresent Chinese-language or Chinese-context studies.. This gap limits the ability to draw robust conclusions from the existing literature. Gap [67b03b55] (medium severity): There are unresolved claim differences across papers under the same task, suggesting a need for clearer comparison criteria.. This gap limits the ability to draw robust conclusions from the existing literature. These gaps collectively indicate that the field lacks the empirical foundation needed to support confident generalization of crossing behavior models across contexts, populations, and technologies. Prioritizing the most severe gaps in future empirical work will be essential for advancing both scientific understanding and the practical applicability of crossing behavior models.

sections.title: Conclusions and Future Research Directions
sections.text: The evidence across 12 reviewed papers converges on a field-level picture in which pedestrian crossing behavior at signalized intersections is influenced by a complex interaction of individual characteristics, traffic flow properties, signal timing parameters, and environmental context. Methodological approaches to modeling this behavior range from structural models grounded in behavioral theory to data-driven approaches optimized for predictive accuracy. Three stable conclusions emerge from this review. First, waiting time is among the most consistent predictors of crossing initiation and violation behavior across reviewed studies. Second, methodological approaches exhibit a persistent tradeoff between interpretability and predictive performance. Third, metric reporting heterogeneity across studies substantially limits the precision of cross-study synthesis. Two persistent tensions run through this literature. The first concerns the cross-context validity of gap acceptance thresholds: values derived from studies in North American and European contexts may not transfer to other traffic environments with different vehicle-pedestrian interaction norms. The second tension concerns the behavioral effects of automated vehicles: whether pedestrians recalibrate their crossing heuristics with sustained AV exposure remains unresolved due to the absence of longitudinal field studies. Resolving these tensions will require longitudinal field studies in diverse cultural contexts. Priority should be given to addressing Gap [0] concerning cross-cultural threshold transfer and Gap [1] concerning longitudinal behavioral adaptation in AV environments — gaps that carry direct implications for both road safety policy and the design of pedestrian-accepting automated vehicle systems.
